coredata.dc:title: Non-pyrolytic synthesis of laccase-like iron based single-atom nanozymes for highly efficient dual-mode colorimetric and fluorescence detection of epinephrine
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Yu-Han
Jiang, Wen-Cai
Zeng, Jing
Wang, Si-Yan
Wu, Wei
Xie, Shu-Dan
Zhao, Yan
Xu, Zhi-Hong
Zhang, Guo-Qi
coredata.dc:description: Single-atom nanozymes have garnered significant attention due to their exceptional atom utilization and ability to establish well-defined structure-activity relationships. However, conventional pyrolytic synthesis methods pose challenges such as high energy consumption and random local environments at the active sites, while achieving non-pyrolytic synthesis of single-atom nanozymes remains a formidable technical hurdle. The present study focuses on the synthesis of laccase-like iron-based single-atom nanozymes (Fe-SAzymes) using a non-pyrolysis method facilitated by microwave irradiation. Under low iron loading conditions, Fe-SAzymes exhibited significantly enhanced laccase activity (12.1 U/mg), surpassing that of laccase by 24-fold. Moreover, Fe-SAzymes demonstrated efficient catalytic oxidation of epinephrine (EP), enabling its colorimetric detection. Owing to the remarkable laccase activity of Fe-SAzymes, the conventional nanozymes EP detection time was reduced from 60 min to 20 min, with an impressive low detection limit as low as 2.95 μM. In addition, an ultra-sensitive fluorescence method for EP detection was developed using the internal filter effect of EP oxidation products and CDs combined with carbon dots probe. The detection limit of fluorescence method was only 0.39 μM. Therefore, an visual, fast, and highly sensitive dual-mode EP detection strategy has great potential in the clinical diagnostic industry.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Non-pyrolytic
Fe-SAzymes
Laccase-like
Epinephrine

Evaluation and determination of laccase-like activity of Fe-SAzymes
Effects of pH or temperature on laccase-like activity of Fe-SAzymes
Evaluation of laccase-like catalytic performance of Fe-SAzymes
Performance of epinephrine detection with colorimetry
Performance of epinephrine detection with fluorometry

We would like to thank Shiyanjia Lab ( www.shiyanjia.com ) for the XPS, SEM and HAADF-STEM testing and analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of the Fe-SAzymes. (A) SEM image of Fe-SAzymes. (B, C) Spherical aberration corrected high-angle annular dark-field scanning transmission electron microscopy (Cs-AC-HAADF-STEM) image and the enlarged image of Fe-SAzymes. (D) The EDS mapping images of Fe-SAzymes. (E) The N2 adsorption and desorption experiments of Fe-SAzymes and pore size distribution of Fe-SAzymes (Inset). (F) Fe 2p fine XPS spectra of Fe-SAzymes. (G) FT-IR and (H) Raman spectra of Fe-SAzymes. (I) XRD spectra of Fe-SAzymes-X (X = 200, 500, 800). XRD PDF standard cards for iron and graphite (Insets).

Laccase-like catalytic performance of Fe-SAzymes. (A) UV–vis absorption spectra in different reaction system (Insets: corresponding photos). (B) Histogram of Fe-SAzymes laccase-like activity with different powers. (C) A wrok curve of laccase-like activity developed by natural laccase. Relative catalytic activity of laccase and Fe-SAzymes towards (D) pH, (E) temperature. (F) Relative catalytic activity of Fe-SAzymes and natural laccase towards storage time.

(A) Verification of the colorimetric assay for EP detection. (B) Verification of the involvement of oxygen in the catalytic reaction. (C) Investigates of repeatability of the proposed colorimetry. (D) The UV–vis spectra of various concentrations of EP catalyzed by Fe-SAzymes. (E) Linear correlation between absorbance response (485 nm) and EP. (F) Selectivity experiments of the proposed colorimetry, the mixture represented the coexistence of above interfering substances.

(A) Verification of the fluorescence assay for EP detection. (B) Excitation and emission scanning curves of CDs and the absorption curve of the EP oxidation products. (C) Fluorescence lifetime diagram of CDs and CDs/EP oxidation products. (D) The fluorescence spectra of CDs quenched by Fe-SAzymes catalyzed EP at different concentrations. (E) Linear correlation between fluorescence response (480 nm) and EP. (F) Selectivity experiments of the proposed fluorometry, the mixture represented the coexistence of above interfering substances.
Schematic illustration of the colorimetric and fluorescent dual-mode for EP detection.
Recovery results obtained for colorimetric channel.
Recovery results obtained for fluorescent channel.

head.ce:title: Non-pyrolytic synthesis of laccase-like iron based single-atom nanozymes for highly efficient dual-mode colorimetric and fluorescence detection of epinephrine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemisty, School of Science, Xihua University, Chengdu, 610039, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemisty, School of Science, Xihua University, Chengdu 610039, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Asymmetric Synthesis and Chiral Technology Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province, Xihua University, Chengdu, 610039, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Asymmetric Synthesis and Chiral Technology Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Asymmetric Synthesis and Chiral Technology Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province, Xihua University, Chengdu 610039, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Chemisty, School of Science, Xihua University, Chengdu, 610039, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Single-atom nanozymes have garnered significant attention due to their exceptional atom utilization and ability to establish well-defined structure-activity relationships. However, conventional pyrolytic synthesis methods pose challenges such as high energy consumption and random local environments at the active sites, while achieving non-pyrolytic synthesis of single-atom nanozymes remains a formidable technical hurdle. The present study focuses on the synthesis of laccase-like iron-based single-atom nanozymes (Fe-SAzymes) using a non-pyrolysis method facilitated by microwave irradiation. Under low iron loading conditions, Fe-SAzymes exhibited significantly enhanced laccase activity (12.1 U/mg), surpassing that of laccase by 24-fold. Moreover, Fe-SAzymes demonstrated efficient catalytic oxidation of epinephrine (EP), enabling its colorimetric detection. Owing to the remarkable laccase activity of Fe-SAzymes, the conventional nanozymes EP detection time was reduced from 60 min to 20 min, with an impressive low detection limit as low as 2.95 μM. In addition, an ultra-sensitive fluorescence method for EP detection was developed using the internal filter effect of EP oxidation products and CDs combined with carbon dots probe. The detection limit of fluorescence method was only 0.39 μM. Therefore, an visual, fast, and highly sensitive dual-mode EP detection strategy has great potential in the clinical diagnostic industry.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Epinephrine (EP), a crucial neurotransmitter, facilitates the transmission of information pertaining to biological activities. Deviations in epinephrine concentration in fluids have been associated with various ailments such as cardiovascular disease and hypertension [1–3]. Moreover, the EP was extensively utilized as an emergency medication for the management of myocardial infarction and anaphylactic shock [4]. Therefore, reaching an accurate determination of the content of EP in fluids holds immense significance for clinical diagnosis and pharmacology.

Recently, a variety of methods have emerged for the detection of EP, including chemiluminescence [5], fluorescence [6], electrochemistry [7] and chromatography [8]. However, in comparison to the aforementioned strategies, colorimetric detection offers the advantages of simplicity in operation, cost-effectiveness, and easy to popularization [9–14]. The rapid advancement of nanozymes technology has garnered significant research funding for the colorimetric detection of EP [15–19]. Lin et al. synthesized iron-based single-atom nanozymes with enhanced laccase activity through high-temperature pyrolysis, enabling the degradation of phenolic pollutants and epinephrine detection. This breakthrough offers new prospects for investigating non-copper center nanozymes with laccase-like activity and their potential applications [20]. Furthermore, Guan et al. employed a host-guest strategy to synthesize Rh single-atom nanozymes with diverse enzyme activities. Additionally, they developed a colorimetric platform for the detection of EP, providing a promising approach for the rational design of highly catalytic and stable nanozymes [21]. Moreover, Zhu et al. developed nitrogen-doped carbon Co/CoOx nanozymes exhibiting laccase-like activity and successfully employed them for the detection of EP in human urine [22]. The analysis speed for EP detection using nanozyme colorimetry was approximately 60 min per sample, which limits its practical applicability. Therefore, developing a synthesis method for highly efficient laccase-like nanozymes could potentially serve as the primary approach to address this issue.

In recent years, single-atom nanozymes have exhibited atomically dispersed metal active sites with remarkable catalytic activity [23–26]. Elucidating the structure-activity relationship requires a well-defined platform. However, conventional synthesis methods involving high temperature pyrolysis result in randomly generated environments surrounding the active sites [27–29], thus necessitating the development of controllable synthetic approaches for achieving atomic-dispersion-level active sites [30–33].

Herein, a novel amorphous Fe-based single-atom nanozymes (Fe-SAzymes) with exceptional laccase-like activity was successfully synthesized using microwave-assisted techniques. The laccase activity of Fe-SAzymes could be significantly modulated by adjusting the microwave power. The laccase activity of Fe-SAzymes, originating from Fe atoms, was confirmed through a KSCN poisoning experiment. The Fe-SAzymes exhibited superior resistance to interference in terms of pH, temperature, and storage duration compared to natural laccase. The Fe-SAzymes was employed as a catalyst for the oxygen oxidation of EP, resulting in its color change from colorless to red. This approach effectively mimicked the activity of laccase and successfully established a robust dual-mode colorimetric and fluorescence
Platform for detecting EP under optimized conditions (Scheme 1 ). The accuracy and applicability of the proposed method were ultimately validated through recovery experiments.
The detail content was provided in Supporting Information(Chemicals and instrumentation).

Fe-SAzymes nanozymes were prepared by following a procedure reported previously with some modifications [34]. In brief, BTC (2 mmol) and ferric chloride (1 mmol) were dissolved in ethylene glycol (25 mL) and ultrasonic for 1 h. Subsequently, DBU (200 μL) was added as a catalyst and allowed to react for 3 min before transferring the reaction mixture to a microwave reactor operating at constant power for 15 min. The mixture was cooled to room temperature, an appropriate 1 M HCl solution was added to precipitate the Fe-SAzymes. The Fe-SAzymes powder were obtained by washing with ethanol and water for several times in turn and freeze-drying in vacuum for 48 h. The laccase-like activity of Fe-SAzymes can be regulated within the microwave power range of 200–800 W. Not specifically stated, Fe-SAzymes indicates that the resultant power was 800W.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of fluorescent probe of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were synthesized in previous literature with slight modification [35]. 0.59 g of succinic acid, 0.50 g of polyethyleneimine, and 0.01 g of FeCl3·6H2O were dissolved in 20 mL ultrapure water. The solution was then transferred to a 25 mL stainless steel reactor and keep at 160 °C for 6 h. Subsequently, the sample was cooled to room temperature and then centrifuged for 5 min. Finally, the supernatant was collected and stored at 4 °C. The obtained CDs concentration is currently measured as 100 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation and determination of laccase-like activity of Fe-SAzymes
ce:section.ce:para: 2,4-DP and 4-AP was employed in this experiment as a chromogenic system for evaluating the laccase-like activity of Fe-SAzymes. First, 4-AP (10 mM, 200 μL), 2,4-DP (10 mM, 200 μL) were added to NaAc-HAc buffer (1 M, pH 6, 1.5 mL). After mixing, Fe-SAzymes (1 mg/mL, 200 μL) were added to the above mixture. The absorption curves or absorbance value at 510 nm was measured after the reaction at 40 °C for 30 min. Utilizing laccase as a standard for constructing a calibration curve to quantify the laccase-like activity of Fe-SAzymes, wherein only the Fe-SAzymes were substituted with natural laccase.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of pH or temperature on laccase-like activity of Fe-SAzymes
ce:section.ce:para: Under the experimental conditions described in section 2.4, the pH and temperature of the buffer were changed to study its effects on laccase-like activity of Fe-SAzymes. To adjust the buffer pH (4–6), the ratio of NaAc and HAc can be modified; alternatively, pH within the range of 7–12 was achieved by regulating the ratio of NaH2PO4 and Na2HPO4. Furthermore, the effect of reaction temperature on the laccase-like of activity was realized by adjusting temperature metal bath (-5-100 ℃). Under the same conditions, the influence of pH and temperature on laccase concentration was 10 mg/mL.

Target 500 μL EP solutions of various concentrations (concentration range of 0.01–1.4 mM) were added to the mixture of 500 μL of 1 mg/mL Fe-SAzymes and 1.5 mL of NaH2PO4–Na2HPO4 (pH 7) and reacted for 20 min at 60 °C. Next, the mixed solution was centrifuged for 3 min under 8000 rpm. The absorption intensity and curve of the liquid supernatant were collected by a UV–vis spectrophotometer. The limit of detection for EP was determined by calculating the ratio of 3 times the standard deviation of the blank signal to the slope of the working curve.

Under the experimental operation described in Section 2.6, the reaction supernatant after centrifugation of 2 mL EP solution of different concentrations (concentration range 0.001–1.5 mM) was mixed with 1 mL CDs (5 %) and quenched at rest for 10 min. The solution was then scanned for fluorescence emission spectra. The calculation of detection limit is the same as 2.6.

The applicability of the developed method in real samples was verified by recovery experiment. Human serum samples were centrifuged with 8000 rpm for 30 min and then diluted 10 times with pure water. Various concentrations of EP were added into the above supernatant and the spiked samples were determined by the proposed method.

The microstructure of Fe-SAzymes was initially examined through SEM measurements. From Fig. 1 A, the surface morphology of Fe-SAzymes exhibits a well-defined pore structure, serving as a conduit for efficient substrate access to the catalytic site. Then, from the spherical aberration corrected high-angle annular dark-field scanning transmission electron microscopy (Cs-AC-HAADF-STEM) images (Fig. 1B) and magnified images (Fig. 1C), it was evident that Fe atoms (highlighted as bright spots) were dispersed across the Fe-SAzymes layer, with no discernible presence of metal clusters, thereby indicating the atomic-level existence of Fe within. And the EDS mapping of Fe-SAzymes and elemental distribution map (Fig. 1D) also revealed its abundant presence of carbon, nitrogen, and iron, which were evenly distributed, providing evidence for the successful preparation of Fe-SAzymes. Furthermore, the Fe-SAzymes were investigated through N2 adsorption and desorption experiments. From Fig. 1E, the observed curve conforms to the characteristics of a Type III isotherm, indicating a weak inter-molecular force between the adsorbent and adsorbate. The results indicated that the presence of Fe-SAzymes exerted a positive influence on the desorption process of the catalytic products. Then, the specific surface area, pore area, total pore volume, and pore diameter of Fe-SAzymes were shown in Fig. 1E and listed in Table S1. The Fe-SAzymes exhibited exceptional porous characteristics, rendering it highly effective in the adsorption of catalytic substrates. The Fe-SAzymes exhibit a pore size ranging from 0.83 to 227.97 nm, which facilitates the ingress and egress of substrate molecules.

The elemental composition and valency of Fe-SAzymes were evaluated through XPS experiments. From the survey full spectrum in Fig. S1A, C 1s, N 1s and Fe 2p can be readily resolved in Fe-SAzymes, with an elemental content of 87.44 at% for C, 11.50 at% for N, and 1.07 at% for Fe. Fig. S1B shows the high-resolution scan of the C 1s electrons where two peaks were deconvoluted at 284.80 and 287.5 eV, due to C–C/C=C, and C=N/C=O, respectively. The fitted peaks for N 1s (Fig. S1C) centered at 398.7 eV and 399.4 eV are attributable to pyridinic and pyrrolic nitrogen, respectively. From Fig. 1F, the Fe 2p spectra present obvious peaks at 709.1 eV, 711.9 eV, 722.2 eV and 725.5 eV assigned to Fe2+ 2p3/2, Fe3+ 2p3/2, Fe2+ 2p1/2and Fe3+ 2p1/2 respectively, which indicating the formation of Fe coordinate sites for active centers. The presence of polyvalent iron in Fe-SAzymes renders it a promising candidate for an efficient electron transfer catalyst.

FT-IR and Raman experiments were then carried out to evaluate the functional group of Fe-SAzymes. As shown in Fig. 1G, the synthesized Fe-SAzymes exhibited a weak peak at 2250 cm−1, indicating the relatively complete BTC reaction. The peaks at.

3350 cm−1 were attributed to –NH and –OH stretching vibrations. Furthermore, the peak detected at 1710 cm−1 corresponds to C=O stretching vibration, while the peak observed at 1510 cm−1 was associated with C=N/C=C. From Fig. 1H of Fe-SAzymes Raman spectrum, the presence of C, N, and Fe in the large ring structure of topological phthalocyanine results in the oscillation of carbon-nitrogen bonds, leading to an absorption peak at 684 cm−1. Additionally, the peak observed at 2235 cm−1 confirms the presence of cyanide groups within the raw material. Significantly, the comparable strengths of the D-band (1334 cm−1) and G-band (1535 cm−1) in Fe-SAzymes suggest a high level of graphitization within the Fe–N/C structure, thereby enhancing electron transfer efficiency. In addition, effect of microwave synthesis power on XRD pattern of Fe-SAzymes was studied. With the increase in microwave power, the crystallinity of Fe-SAzymes gradually diminishes, ultimately resulting in a fully amorphous state (Fig. 1I). Notably, the XRD patterns of Fe-SAzymes-500 and Fe-SAzymes-800 do not exhibit any distinguishable diffraction peaks corresponding to iron or its metal oxides. Furthermore, upon comparing Fe-Sazyms-800 with XRD PDF cards of graphite and iron, it is observed that the material possesses crystal faces similar to graphite (002), but lacks crystal faces resembling iron (112)，indicating that high microwave power promotes the presence of atomic dispersion of Fe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of laccase-like catalytic performance of Fe-SAzymes
ce:section.ce:para: The laccase-like activity of Fe-SAzymes was evaluated by the color reaction of 2,4-DP and 4-AP. In simple terms, lachase acts as a catalyst for the oxygen oxidation of 2,4-DP and 4-AP condensation reaction, resulting in the formation of a wine red product with a distinct absorption peak at 510 nm. From Fig. 2 A, Fe-SAzymes effectively catalyzed the production of red products in a short period of time and exhibited well-defined absorption peaks at 510 nm in both 2,4-DP and 4-AP reaction systems. This observation implies that Fe-SAzymes exhibit laccase-like characteristics. Moreover, effect of microwave synthesis power on the laccase-like activity of Fe-SAzymes was investigated in Fig. 2B. The application of high microwave power was conducive to enhancing the laccase-like activity of Fe-SAzymes, potentially attributed to the increased atomization degree of Fe, which aligns with the findings depicted in Fig. 1I. The underlying mechanism governing the impact of microwave power on the catalytic activity of nanozymes will be comprehensively elucidated in a separate publication. A working curve of enzyme activity was established using natural laccase as a reference (Fig. 2C). By conversion, 1 mg Fe-SAzymes corresponded to 12.1 U, which was 24-fold higher than that of natural laccase. In addition to exhibiting enhanced catalytic activity, Fe-SAzymes demonstrate superior tolerance towards variations in acidity (Fig. 2D) and highly temperature (Fig. 2E) compared to their natural laccase counterparts. In this study, the steady-state dynamics of Fe-SAzymes was studied. After performing calculations, the Michaelis-Menten constant (K m) and maximum reaction rate (V max) of Fe-SAzymes for 2,4-DP were determined to be 0.397 mM and 1.114·10−5 mMs−1, respectively (Fig. S2). Compared with natural laccase and other nanozymes, Fe-SAzymes occupy a lower K m, indicating a stronger affinity for the substrate (Table S2). Then, the storage.

stability of Fe-SAzymes and natural laccase (Fig. 2F) were investigated, Fe-SAzymes exhibit remarkable storage stability, maintaining their catalytic activity unchanged for up to 10 days, while laccase gradually loses its activity and becomes inactive. The above results suggest that Fe-SAzymes possesses superior catalytic performance compared to natural laccase. The strong coordination between KSCN and Fe leads to an increase in the concentration of KSCN, resulting in a gradual reduction in the laccase-like catalytic activity of Fe-Sazymes (Fig. S3). This observation suggests that Fe serves as the catalytic site for laccase activity in Fe-Sazymes, which is distinct from the copper-based active site found in natural laccases. Furthermore, the concentration-dependent regulation of laccase-like activity in Fe-SAzymes highlights its potential as an optimal candidate for developing analytical methods for EP (Fig. S4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of epinephrine detection with colorimetry
ce:section.ce:para: UV–vis spectrophotometer was utilized to examine the feasibility of EP detection with colorimetry. As displayed in Fig. 3 A, an obvious absorption at 485 nm appears and the color of the mixture changes to rosy red (inset in Fig. 3A). Nitrogen was injected into the catalytic reaction system to reduce the oxygen content, which obviously inhibited the efficiency of Fe-SAzymes catalyzing EP (Fig. 3B). This indicates the fact that Fe-SAzymes catalyzes O2 oxidation of EP.

Subsequently, effects of pH, temperature, reaction time and concentration of Fe-SAzymes on the detection of EP were studied. The optimal detection conditions were determined considering linearity and sensitivity, with the following parameters: pH of 7, temperature of 60 °C, reaction time of 20 min, and Fe-SAzymes concentration of 1 mg/mL (Fig. S5). The stability of Fe-SAzymes determination of EP was assessed by analyzing EP (0.8 mM) as the sample for seven replicates, yielding a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 2.4 % (Fig. 3C). This result indicates that the strategy exhibits satisfactory stability. Fig. 3D shows that the absorption spectral curves of adrenochrome (EP oxidation products) varies with EP concentration. When the EP concentration was between 0.01 and 1.4 mM, the absorbance intensity increased linearly with the EP concentration, A = 0.7102 C EP - 0.0032 (r = 0.9998), with a LOD of about 2.95 μM (Fig. 3E). Notably, compared to other literates, the proposed colorimetric sensing EP exhibits equivalent sensitivity while significantly enhancing EP detection efficiency (Table S3). The anti-interfering ability of the proposed method was validated using potential coexisting substance, including dopamine (DA).

glucose (Glc), glutamic acid (Glu), glycine (Gly), uric acid (UA), ascorbic acid (AsA), cysteine (L-Cys), glutathione (GSH), KCl, and NaCl. As from Fig. 3F and Fig. S6, the influence of interfering substances on the detection of EP absorbance intensity could be disregarded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of epinephrine detection with fluorometry
ce:section.ce:para: The integration of dual-mode sensing technology leverages the inherent advantages of both sensing mechanisms, thereby enhancing the performance, reliability, and adaptability to complex environments in sensing systems [36]. Enhancing the fluorescence signal channel was commonly achieved by leveraging the interaction between a fluorescent probe and a colored substance. Among these, carbon dots emerge as a promising fluorescent probe due to their facile synthesis, versatile design options, and exceptional luminescent properties. There has been a limited number of fluorescence sensing strategies employed for EP detection using nanozymes. Therefore, the construction of fluorescent channels for EP detection using nanozymes and carbon dots presents a creative challenge in this study. Following the screening process, it was observed that the catalytic products of EP possess the ability to quench fluorescence of carbon dots (the preparation process was outlined in section 2.3), exhibiting a potential linear correlation with EP concentration (Fig. 4 A). The morphology of the prepared CDs were investigated using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), as shown in Fig. S7. The prepared CDs exhibited highly dispersed quasi-spherical structures, with particle sizes below 10 nm. FT-IR and XPS spectra were analyzed for the surface functional groups of CDs. FT-IR exhibited the surface of CDs contained a large number of O–H/N–H, C=O and C–O groups (Fig. S8). The full XPS spectra revealed that contained C, N, O and Fe elements in CDs (Fig. S9). As from Fig. 4B, it has been suggested that the quenching effect observed when the absorption spectrum of the quencher overlaps with either the excitation or emission spectrum of CDs could be attributed to internal filtration effect (IFE) or foster resonance energy transfer (FRET). Consequently, we conducted a comparative analysis between the fluorescence spectra of CDs (480 nm) and the absorption spectra (485 nm) of EP oxidation products, which revealed a significant spectral overlap. As from Fig. 4C, the fluorescence lifetime of CDs was minimally affected by the EP oxidation products, suggesting that CDs quenching was due to inner filter effect rather than resonance energy transfer. Through 7 parallel experiments, the results were relatively stable (RSD 2.62 %), which indicates that the method has good repeatability (Fig. S10). Under the optimal EP catalytic oxidation condition, the fluorescence detection method of EP was investigated, taking into account the incubation time and concentrations of CDs (Fig. S11). Fig. 4D shows the relevant fluorescence spectra of various amounts of EP. The fluorescence signal decreased with the increase EP concentrations ranging from 0 to 1.5 mM. The fluorescence intensity at 480 nm correlated linearly with the EP concentration from 0.001 mM to 1.0 mM with an low detection limit of 0.39 μM (Fig. 4E). The corresponding linear equation was F = −461369 C EP + 920674 (r = 0.9962). Besides, the selectivity of the method has also been studied by fluorescence method (Fig. 4F and Fig. S12). The potential interfering substances exhibit negligible impact. Obviously, a more sensitive fluorescent channel for EP detection has been successfully developed.

To evaluate the practicality in real-world scenarios, we adopted the proposed dual-mode approach to detect EP in human serum. EP was diluted for quantitative detection via this dual-mode method. The human blood sample underwent pretreatment according to a standardized protocol to obtain serum sample. The treated serum sample with added EP was subsequently subjected to developed dual-mode method with a similar manner above. The recovery obtained in Tables 1 and 2
falls within the acceptable range (Colorimetry: 95.16 %–109.29 %; Fluorometry: 96.25 %–106.23 %), demonstrating the feasibility of the proposed dual-mode approach for accurate detection of EP in real samples.

As summarized, a iron-based single-atom nanozymes (Fe-SAzymes) with outstanding laccase-like activity was prepared using a non-pyrolytic microwave method. The laccase activity of Fe-SAzymes was regulated by microwave power. we have developed a dual-mode colorimetry and fluorometry for the detection of EP based on the discoloration reaction of EP catalyzed by Fe-SAzymes. The fluorescence signal was acquired via the inner filter effect between the oxidation products of EP and a fluorescent probe (carbon dots). The novel dual-mode method provides excellent sensitivity and efficiency for the quantitative detection of EP. The dual-mode method provides two independent detection signals that could be cross-validated for accurate and high-fidelity results. The proposed method demonstrates direct detection of EP in human serum, thus providing promising prospects for clinical diagnosis. Simultaneously, it offers an avenue for investigating the laccase-like activity of single-atom nanozymes.

Yu-Han Shi: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation. Wen-Cai Jiang: Methodology, Investigation. Jing Zeng: Validation, Investigation. Si-Yan Wang: Validation, Investigation. Wei Wu: Investigation. Shu-Dan Xie: Investigation. Yan Zhao: Resources, Project administration. Zhi-Hong Xu: Project administration. Guo-Qi Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We appreciate the financial support from the Sichuan Science and Technology Program No. 2023NSFSC0637, Xihua University Talent Introduction Program (No. RX2200001988), the Quality Project of Graduate Education, Xihua University (No. YK20240192, YKC202407 and YK20240181). We would like to thank Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the XPS, SEM and HAADF-STEM testing and analysis.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2024.343031.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chemical sensing of neurotransmitters
Chem. Soc. Rev.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Pradhan, H.S. Jung, J.H. Jang T.W. Kim, C. Kang, J.S. Kim, Chemical sensing of neurotransmitters, Chem. Soc. Rev. 43 (2014) 4684-4713.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical sensors and biosensors for determination of catecholamine neurotransmitters: a review
Talanta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.A. Ribeiro, P.M.V. Fernandes, C.M. Pereira, F. Silva, Electrochemical sensors and biosensors for determination of catecholamine neurotransmitters: a review, Talanta 160 (2016) 653-679.
